content:
3 min read
Just now

--

A quick experiment in building lightweight single-page web tools and games without heavy frameworks.
Every web developer reaches a point where they get tired of npm install pulling in 200MB of node_modules just to render a simple UI.
Recently, I’ve been exploring the concept of building lightweight, single-page web applications and mini-games. The goal is simple: create fully functional, zero-dependency tools using standard modern web technologies (HTML5 Canvas, CSS grid/flexbox, and plain JavaScript).
To kick off this experiment, I built a classic Snake Game. Here is a breakdown of how it works under the hood, how I handled touch/swipe controls for mobile, and the core rendering loop.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Amanz on UnsplashThe Architecture: Keeping It Dependency-Free
The entire game runs inside a single <canvas> element (400x400), managed by a basic tick loop. Instead of importing external game engines like Phaser, standard Canvas API calls handle rendering at designated intervals based on selected speed (Slow, Normal, Fast).
Canvas (400x400)
├── Grid System (20x20px blocks)
├── Snake Array [{x, y}, {x, y}, ...]
└── Dynamic Render Loop (setInterval based on speed)
